segments.text: Now, we're going to take a look at the
actual product. So, what you're seeing
here is the summary of the main branch
view with some details about the last
analysis that we ran. Um, there are 26
dependency risks that have shown up. So,
I'll go ahead and click into this view.
This is showing me everything that we
would expect to be out in production
right now. It's on the main branch. Um,
but what you can do here is instrument
quality gates to only show your
developers newly introduced risks. um or
you can elect to take that holistic view
um for your main branch or for any
branch. So the risk types that we have
here are vulnerabilities and prohibited
licenses. Um you can filter uh sort by
these types of views and you can also
use risk severity as a way to understand
quickly what's going on in your project.
Um so we're going to go ahead and take a
look at a specific um vulnerability
here.
Um, so this is an example of one of our
risks and what we're showing is the
description of the risk itself. Um,
we're giving the developer steps to take
to fix this risk, letting them know the
particular version that they're on
today. Um, where some partial fixes are
available as they move up um, in this
case along minor releases and that the
complete fix is looks like about seven
minor releases away.
Um this is a direct package so there's
no um dependency tree for how this is
coming in. Um but were there would there
be one you could click through here and
see that um and then you can also do the
same practice of changing the status
that we see on other kinds of code
quality issues or static analysis issues
in sonar Q. So developers can change the
status here. Um they may have specific
context to the project where they're
determining that there's no risk posed.
um they may accept it but know that they
won't be able to make a change soon. Um
or it may be that this is confirmed and
the intent there is to prioritize this
work quickly
regardless of what the decision is. The
developer can leave a note here
explaining um what what the note is
about and this creates better
auditability across your organization
for what's going on at any given point
in time.
One more thing I want to call out here.
Um this is actually an example of a
package where we have a um contractual
relationship in place with the
maintainer behind the package. And so in
this instance um there is a deeper uh
sort of post-mortem review on the CVE
that the maintainer contributes. Um it
will have additional information about
any potential workarounds. um anytime
there's a false positive, they'll call
that out and and the reasons behind
that. Um so it's just more kind of
context information from the person who
actually wrote the software in this
dependency that helps developers um take
action more quickly or more
strategically in a more informed manner.
I'm going to go back to my dependency
list here
um and take off this filter. Let's take
a look at a prohibited license one. So,
in this case, we've got a license that
has an LGPL
um with an additional additional sort of
exception text on the on the SPDX
expression. Um so, this is currently
prohibited by my licensing profile. Um
so again very helpful for developers to
see this um in the workflows they're
already using for code quality for code
security issues um and not have to
juggle different tooling.
Um we're also providing the software
bill of materials so you can search for
a particular dependency name. Um you can
also export a machine readable format of
your full dependency list. And there's a
couple of different filtering uh options
available here as well. Um
I also wanted to quickly show that you
can configure quality gates um based on
your dependency risks.
So to do that I will create my gate and
then I will add a condition
and I might want to say um I do want to
fail a gate on new code anytime um a
vulnerability comes in that's a high or
greater. Um so this is a condition that
I add and now um this will be applied to
any project that falls under this
quality gate licensing profiles a little
bit more of a specialized version or
specialized configuration. So again I
can create this here.
I can go in and manage um licenses and I
can go in one by one and say my policy
allows or prohibits this or I can use
some of our starter templates that give
me a quick way to say we're going to
allow um permissive licenses as an
example and maybe we're okay with weak
copy lift.
And a really great feature of this is
you can actually apply this to a
specific subset of projects if you want.
So, if you've got something that's more
of an internal application, you're not
necessarily worried about open source
licensing. Um, you can choose to leave
that out of a more strict policy. Um, if
you're doing mobile application
development or you have something that's
distributed application, that's where
you're going to want to be more
sensitive to um licensing uh
configuration and licensing compliance
enforcement. Um, so it's nice to have
that that option. And you can create um
multiple flavors of this as well, which
is also helpful if you are in a moment
where you want to take a graduated
approach to rolling this out to your
developers. Um you know, if you have
never dealt with open source licensing
before, you may want to start with sort
of the more most egregious types of
licensing um license violations that
could happen and go from there.
Um so this is just a quick overview.
really hope that folks are connecting
with us online and letting us know what
you think.

fullText: Now, we're going to take a look at the actual product. So, what you're seeing here is the summary of the main branch view with some details about the last analysis that we ran. Um, there are 26 dependency risks that have shown up. So, I'll go ahead and click into this view. This is showing me everything that we would expect to be out in production right now. It's on the main branch. Um, but what you can do here is instrument quality gates to only show your developers newly introduced risks. um or you can elect to take that holistic view um for your main branch or for any branch. So the risk types that we have here are vulnerabilities and prohibited licenses. Um you can filter uh sort by these types of views and you can also use risk severity as a way to understand quickly what's going on in your project. Um so we're going to go ahead and take a look at a specific um vulnerability here. Um, so this is an example of one of our risks and what we're showing is the description of the risk itself. Um, we're giving the developer steps to take to fix this risk, letting them know the particular version that they're on today. Um, where some partial fixes are available as they move up um, in this case along minor releases and that the complete fix is looks like about seven minor releases away. Um this is a direct package so there's no um dependency tree for how this is coming in. Um but were there would there be one you could click through here and see that um and then you can also do the same practice of changing the status that we see on other kinds of code quality issues or static analysis issues in sonar Q. So developers can change the status here. Um they may have specific context to the project where they're determining that there's no risk posed. um they may accept it but know that they won't be able to make a change soon. Um or it may be that this is confirmed and the intent there is to prioritize this work quickly regardless of what the decision is. The developer can leave a note here explaining um what what the note is about and this creates better auditability across your organization for what's going on at any given point in time. One more thing I want to call out here. Um this is actually an example of a package where we have a um contractual relationship in place with the maintainer behind the package. And so in this instance um there is a deeper uh sort of post-mortem review on the CVE that the maintainer contributes. Um it will have additional information about any potential workarounds. um anytime there's a false positive, they'll call that out and and the reasons behind that. Um so it's just more kind of context information from the person who actually wrote the software in this dependency that helps developers um take action more quickly or more strategically in a more informed manner. I'm going to go back to my dependency list here um and take off this filter. Let's take a look at a prohibited license one. So, in this case, we've got a license that has an LGPL um with an additional additional sort of exception text on the on the SPDX expression. Um so, this is currently prohibited by my licensing profile. Um so again very helpful for developers to see this um in the workflows they're already using for code quality for code security issues um and not have to juggle different tooling. Um we're also providing the software bill of materials so you can search for a particular dependency name. Um you can also export a machine readable format of your full dependency list. And there's a couple of different filtering uh options available here as well. Um I also wanted to quickly show that you can configure quality gates um based on your dependency risks. So to do that I will create my gate and then I will add a condition and I might want to say um I do want to fail a gate on new code anytime um a vulnerability comes in that's a high or greater. Um so this is a condition that I add and now um this will be applied to any project that falls under this quality gate licensing profiles a little bit more of a specialized version or specialized configuration. So again I can create this here. I can go in and manage um licenses and I can go in one by one and say my policy allows or prohibits this or I can use some of our starter templates that give me a quick way to say we're going to allow um permissive licenses as an example and maybe we're okay with weak copy lift. And a really great feature of this is you can actually apply this to a specific subset of projects if you want. So, if you've got something that's more of an internal application, you're not necessarily worried about open source licensing. Um, you can choose to leave that out of a more strict policy. Um, if you're doing mobile application development or you have something that's distributed application, that's where you're going to want to be more sensitive to um licensing uh configuration and licensing compliance enforcement. Um, so it's nice to have that that option. And you can create um multiple flavors of this as well, which is also helpful if you are in a moment where you want to take a graduated approach to rolling this out to your developers. Um you know, if you have never dealt with open source licensing before, you may want to start with sort of the more most egregious types of licensing um license violations that could happen and go from there. Um so this is just a quick overview. really hope that folks are connecting with us online and letting us know what you think.
